message.content:
The bell above the door jingled, cutting through the symphony of ticking that filled the shop. It wasn’t a single sound, but a layered rhythm—hundreds of clocks, each with its own heartbeat, creating a chaotic yet harmonious hum.

Leo, a boy of about eight with knees stained from grass and pockets full of pebbles, stepped inside. He blinked, adjusting to the dim, amber light. Behind a counter cluttered with gears, springs, and magnifying glasses sat Mr. Abernathy. The old man looked as though he had been carved from the same oak as the grandfather clocks lining the walls. His fingers were stained with oil, and his eyes, magnified by thick spectacles, seemed to focus on things far smaller than the room they shared.

"Closed," Mr. Abernathy said, not looking up from the tiny escapement he was adjusting with tweezers.

"I didn't see a sign," Leo said, his voice barely rising above the tick-tock. "And I didn't want to buy anything. I just... I wanted to ask you something."

Mr. Abernathy paused. He lowered the tweezers slowly. "Questions are expensive, boy. They cost attention. And my attention is currently occupied by this mainspring."

"It’s about time," Leo said.

The clockmaker sighed, a dry sound like rustling leaves. "Time is what I sell. I sell hours, minutes, seconds. I don’t sell philosophy. Go home."

"But isn't it strange?" Leo pressed, stepping closer to the counter. "Outside, time feels like a river. It just flows. But in here... it feels like it’s jumping. Tick. Tick. Tick. Like it’s broken into pieces. If time is real, why does it have to be chopped up?"

Mr. Aber
